Netflix Business Case: Data Exploration and Visualisation¶

Introduction¶

This project analyzes Netflix’s content catalog to identify patterns in the type of content available on the platform and generate business insights that can help Netflix make better decisions about what kind of shows or movies to produce and how to grow its business across different countries.

Netflix is one of the world’s most popular media and video streaming platforms. As of mid-2021, it had over 222 million subscribers globally and offered a large catalog of movies and TV shows. The dataset used in this case study contains information about titles available on Netflix, including details such as:

  • show type
  • title
  • director
  • cast
  • country
  • date added
  • release year
  • rating
  • duration
  • genre
  • description

Business Problem¶

The goal of this case study is to analyze Netflix’s catalog and generate data-driven insights that can help answer questions such as:

  • What type of content is available in different countries?
  • How has the number of movies released per year changed over time?
  • How do TV shows compare with movies on the platform?
  • What might be the best time to launch a TV show?
  • Which actors and directors appear most often in different types of content?
  • Has Netflix shifted focus toward TV shows in recent years?
  • What patterns in content distribution can help Netflix grow in international markets?

Objective of the Analysis¶

The main objectives of this project are:

  1. Understand the structure and quality of the dataset
  2. Perform data cleaning and preprocessing
  3. Conduct non-graphical and visual exploratory data analysis
  4. Identify important content trends across type, country, release year, rating, genre, actors, and directors
  5. Generate business insights from the observed patterns
  6. Provide simple and actionable recommendations for Netflix leadership

Dataset Description¶

The dataset contains the following columns:

  • show_id: Unique ID for every movie or TV show
  • type: Whether the title is a Movie or TV Show
  • title: Name of the movie or TV show
  • director: Director of the title
  • cast: Actors involved in the title
  • country: Country where the content was produced
  • date_added: Date the content was added to Netflix
  • release_year: Actual release year of the content
  • rating: TV/content rating
  • duration: Duration in minutes or number of seasons
  • listed_in: Genre/category of the content
  • description: Summary description of the title

Approach¶

To solve this business problem, this notebook will follow these steps:

  1. Import libraries and load the dataset
  2. Understand the shape, columns, data types, and missing values
  3. Perform preprocessing and feature cleanup
  4. Conduct non-graphical analysis
  5. Perform univariate and bivariate visual analysis
  6. Check missing values and outliers
  7. Summarize key business insights
  8. Provide final recommendations for Netflix

Evaluation-Focused Note¶

This notebook is designed as a business-facing case study. The analysis will focus not only on technical exploration, but also on clear interpretation, practical insights, and recommendations that can be understood by business stakeholders without relying on heavy technical jargon.

In [3]:
# Import Libraries and Test Setup
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.max_rows', 200)

print("All libraries imported successfully.")

All libraries imported successfully.

Initial Observations¶

The dataset has been loaded successfully and contains 8,807 rows and 12 columns.

Structure of the dataset¶

  • The dataset includes information about Netflix titles such as:
    • content type
    • title
    • director
    • cast
    • country
    • date added
    • release year
    • rating
    • duration
    • genre
    • description

Data type observations¶

  • Most columns are currently stored as string/object type
  • Only release_year is currently in numeric format (int64)
  • Columns such as date_added will need to be converted into proper datetime format during preprocessing
  • Some categorical columns may later be converted to category type for cleaner analysis

Missing value observations¶

There are visible missing values in multiple columns, especially:

  • director
  • cast
  • country
  • date_added
  • rating
  • duration

This indicates that data cleaning and preprocessing will be an important part of the analysis before moving into non-graphical and visual exploration.

Next step¶

The next step is to perform a more detailed data quality check, including:

  • missing value analysis
  • duplicate row check
  • summary statistics
  • unique value counts
  • data type review and preprocessing preparation

Observations from Data Quality Checks and Summary Statistics¶

Based on the initial exploration, the dataset contains 8,807 titles and 12 columns, which provides a reasonably large sample for analyzing Netflix’s content catalog.

1. Dataset structure¶

  • The dataset includes both movies and TV shows
  • There are no duplicate rows, which means we do not need to perform duplicate removal at this stage
  • Most columns are stored as object/string type, while release_year is the main numeric column currently available for direct quantitative analysis

2. Missing value observations¶

The dataset contains missing values in a few important columns:

  • director has the highest missingness: 2,634 missing values (~29.91%)
  • country has 831 missing values (~9.44%)
  • cast has 825 missing values (~9.37%)
  • date_added, rating, and duration have only a very small number of missing values

Interpretation¶

The high percentage of missing values in director suggests that this column may be incomplete for a significant portion of titles, especially for certain content categories or regions. The missing values in country and cast are also important because both columns are directly relevant to business questions related to international expansion and talent analysis.

3. Summary-level observations¶

  • type contains only 2 categories, which confirms that the catalog is split into Movies and TV Shows
  • show_id and title appear to behave like identifier fields
  • country, director, cast, and listed_in have high-cardinality text values, which indicates that preprocessing and unnesting will be required before detailed analysis
  • release_year ranges from 1925 to 2021, showing that Netflix contains both older titles and very recent releases
  • The median release year is relatively recent, suggesting that much of the catalog is concentrated in modern content
  • The most common content rating appears to be TV-MA
  • The duration column includes mixed formats such as minutes for movies and seasons for TV shows, so this feature will need to be separated into cleaner components before analysis

4. Key preprocessing implications¶

Before moving into deeper visual and business analysis, the following preprocessing steps will be necessary:

  • convert date_added into datetime format
  • clean and split the duration column
  • unnest columns such as country, director, cast, and listed_in
  • review how missing values should be handled for key business variables

5. Business relevance¶

These early observations already suggest that the dataset is suitable for business analysis, but it also shows that raw data cannot be interpreted directly without cleaning. In particular, fields related to country, director, cast, and duration will play an important role in answering Netflix’s strategic questions around content production, content mix, and international growth.

Data Cleaning and Preprocessing¶

Before starting non-graphical and visual analysis, the dataset needs to be cleaned and prepared.

This preprocessing stage focuses on:

  • converting date columns into proper datetime format
  • extracting useful time-based features
  • cleaning the duration column
  • checking unique values in important categorical columns
  • preparing multi-value columns such as country, director, cast, and listed_in for deeper analysis

These steps are necessary because several variables are stored as raw text and cannot be analyzed properly in their current form.

In [11]:
# Create a copy of the original dataset for preprocessing
df_clean = df.copy()

print("Working copy created successfully.")

Working copy created successfully.
In [12]:
# Convert date_added to datetime
df_clean['date_added'] = pd.to_datetime(df_clean['date_added'], errors='coerce')

df_clean[['date_added']].head()
Out[12]:
date_added
0 2021-09-25
1 2021-09-24
2 2021-09-24
3 2021-09-24
4 2021-09-24
In [13]:
# Extract year_added and month_added from date_added
df_clean['year_added'] = df_clean['date_added'].dt.year
df_clean['month_added'] = df_clean['date_added'].dt.month_name()

df_clean[['date_added', 'year_added', 'month_added']].head()
Out[13]:

0 2021-09-25 2021.0 September
1 2021-09-24 2021.0 September
2 2021-09-24 2021.0 September
3 2021-09-24 2021.0 September
4 2021-09-24 2021.0 September

In [15]:
# Split duration into numeric value and unit
df_clean[['duration_int', 'duration_type']] = df_clean['duration'].str.extract(r'(\d+)\s*(\w+)')

df_clean['duration_int'] = pd.to_numeric(df_clean['duration_int'], errors='coerce')

df_clean[['duration', 'duration_int', 'duration_type']].head(10)

5 1 Season 1.0 Season
6 91 min 91.0 min
7 125 min 125.0 min
8 9 Seasons 9.0 Seasons
9 104 min 104.0 min

Observations from Preprocessing¶

Several important preprocessing steps were completed to make the dataset ready for deeper exploratory and business analysis.

1. Date conversion and feature extraction¶

  • The date_added column was successfully converted from string format into datetime
  • Two additional time-based features were created:
    • year_added
    • month_added

These new variables will help analyze when content was added to Netflix and identify possible seasonal or yearly platform trends.

2. Duration cleaning¶

  • The original duration column contained mixed formats:
    • movie durations in minutes
    • TV show durations in seasons
  • To make this column analyzable, it was split into:
    • duration_int
    • duration_type

After standardization, the dataset shows:

  • 6128 titles with duration measured in Minutes
  • 2676 titles with duration measured in Seasons
  • 3 records with missing duration information

This confirms that the dataset contains a much larger number of movie-style durations than season-based durations, which is consistent with Netflix having more movies than TV shows.

3. Categorical variable review¶

The number of unique values in some important categorical variables is as follows:

  • type → 2 unique values
  • rating → 17 unique values
  • country → 748 unique values
  • director → 4528 unique values
  • cast → 7692 unique values
  • listed_in → 514 unique values

This shows that country, director, cast, and listed_in are high-cardinality columns, and these variables will require splitting and unnesting before detailed actor-level, country-level, director-level, or genre-level analysis.

4. Data type improvements¶

The following columns were converted to category type for cleaner and more efficient analysis:

  • type
  • rating
  • duration_type
  • month_added

In addition, the cleaned dataset now contains 16 columns, compared to the original 12 columns, because of the newly created preprocessing features.

5. Why this preprocessing matters¶

These preprocessing steps were necessary because the raw dataset could not directly support high-quality business analysis. After cleaning, the data is now better structured for answering questions related to:

  • movie vs TV show comparisons
  • launch timing patterns
  • country-wise content distribution
  • rating and genre trends
  • business recommendations for Netflix content strategy

Non-Graphical Analysis¶

After cleaning the data, the next step is to perform non-graphical analysis.

The purpose of this section is to understand the dataset using summary tables, counts, and frequency-based observations before moving into visual analysis.

This section focuses on:

  • comparing movies and TV shows
  • understanding release year patterns
  • identifying the most common ratings
  • identifying the most frequent genres, countries, directors, and cast members
  • highlighting early business patterns in Netflix’s catalog

# Explode country column for country-level analysis
df_country = df_clean[['show_id', 'type', 'country']].dropna().copy()
df_country['country'] = df_country['country'].str.split(', ')
df_country = df_country.explode('country')

Observations from Non-Graphical Analysis¶

The non-graphical analysis provides a strong first view of how Netflix’s catalog is structured before moving into chart-based analysis.

1. Content type mix¶

The catalog is clearly dominated by Movies.

  • Movies: 6131 titles (69.62%)
  • TV Shows: 2676 titles (30.38%)

This indicates that Netflix’s library is still more movie-heavy than TV-show-heavy, although later time-based analysis will help determine whether TV shows have grown more rapidly in recent years.

2. Release year distribution¶

The release year summary shows that Netflix’s content is heavily concentrated in relatively recent years.

  • Minimum release year: 1925
  • Maximum release year: 2021
  • Mean release year: about 2014
  • Median release year: 2017
  • 75th percentile: 2019

This suggests that although some older titles exist, most of Netflix’s catalog is made up of modern content, especially titles released in the last decade.

3. Titles added to Netflix over time¶

The year_added counts suggest that Netflix added very little content in the earlier years and then expanded rapidly in later years. This likely reflects Netflix’s aggressive growth in content acquisition and global expansion in the second half of the 2010s.

4. Ratings pattern¶

The most common rating is clearly TV-MA, followed by TV-14.

Some of the most frequent ratings are:

  • TV-MA → 3207
  • TV-14 → 2160
  • TV-PG → 863
  • R → 799
  • PG-13 → 490

This suggests that a large portion of Netflix’s catalog targets a more mature audience, while still maintaining a meaningful amount of family and teen-accessible content.

5. Genre pattern¶

After splitting and unnesting the genre column, the most frequent genres include:

  • International Movies → 2752
  • Dramas → 2427
  • Comedies → 1674
  • International TV Shows → 1351
  • Documentaries → 869
  • Action & Adventure → 859
  • TV Dramas → 763

This indicates that Netflix has a strong emphasis on:

  • international content
  • drama-based storytelling
  • comedy
  • documentary and action-based categories

The strong presence of international genres suggests that Netflix’s catalog strategy is highly global rather than purely US-centered.

6. Country pattern¶

After exploding the country column, the top content-producing countries in the dataset are:

  • United States → 3689
  • India → 1046
  • United Kingdom → 804
  • Canada → 445
  • France → 393
  • Japan → 318
  • Spain → 232
  • South Korea → 231
  • Germany → 226
  • Mexico → 169

This shows that Netflix content is highly concentrated in a few major markets, especially the United States, with India and the United Kingdom also playing major roles. It also suggests strong international catalog depth in Europe and Asia.

7. Director pattern¶

After exploding the director column, the most frequently appearing directors include:

  • Rajiv Chilaka → 22
  • Jan Suter → 21
  • Raúl Campos → 19
  • Suhas Kadav → 16
  • Marcus Raboy → 16

This suggests that some directors appear repeatedly in Netflix’s catalog, possibly indicating strong production partnerships or recurring work in specific content niches.

8. Cast pattern¶

After exploding the cast column, the most frequently appearing actors include:

  • Anupam Kher → 43
  • Shah Rukh Khan → 35
  • Julie Tejwani → 33
  • Naseeruddin Shah → 32
  • Takahiro Sakurai → 32
  • Rupa Bhimani → 31
  • Akshay Kumar → 30
  • Om Puri → 30
  • Yuki Kaji → 29
  • Amitabh Bachchan → 28

A noticeable point here is the strong presence of Indian actors, which may reflect the large contribution of Indian content to Netflix’s global library.

9. Duration pattern¶

The duration summaries show a clear structural difference between movies and TV shows:

Movies¶

  • Count: 6128
  • Mean duration: about 99.58 minutes
  • Median duration: 98 minutes
  • 75th percentile: 114 minutes
  • Maximum: 312 minutes

This suggests that most Netflix movies fall within a fairly standard feature-length range.

TV Shows¶

  • Count: 2676
  • Mean seasons: about 1.76
  • Median seasons: 1
  • 75th percentile: 2
  • Maximum: 17 seasons

This indicates that most TV shows on Netflix are relatively short-running, with one-season and two-season shows being the most common.

10. Business relevance¶

These non-graphical findings already highlight several important business patterns:

  • Netflix’s catalog is still dominated by movies
  • the platform is heavily focused on recent content
  • mature ratings such as TV-MA and TV-14 dominate the platform
  • international content is a major pillar of the catalog
  • a few countries, especially the US, India, and UK, contribute a large share of titles
  • most TV shows are relatively short, which may reflect lower risk, easier renewals, or a preference for compact storytelling formats

These patterns provide a strong foundation for the visual analysis section, where the same themes can be explored more clearly through plots and comparisons.

Visual Analysis¶

After completing the non-graphical analysis, the next step is to use visualizations to better understand the distribution of Netflix content and the relationships between key variables.

This section focuses on:

  • univariate analysis of important variables
  • bivariate analysis across content type, year, country, genre, and rating
  • identifying patterns in release timing
  • checking the spread of movie durations and TV show seasons
  • supporting business insights with visual evidence

The goal is not just to create plots, but to use them to answer Netflix’s business questions in a simple and interpretable way.

In [40]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
sns.countplot(data=df_clean, x='type')
plt.title('Distribution of Content Type on Netflix')
plt.xlabel('Content Type')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observation¶

Movies clearly dominate the Netflix catalog compared to TV Shows.

Interpretation¶

This confirms that Netflix’s library is still more movie-heavy. However, this does not yet tell us whether TV Shows are becoming more important in recent years, which will be explored later using time-based analysis.

In [41]:
# Release Year Distribution
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.histplot(df_clean['release_year'], bins=30, kde=True)
plt.title('Distribution of Release Year')
plt.xlabel('Release Year')
plt.ylabel('Number of Titles')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observation¶

Most titles in the dataset are concentrated in relatively recent years, with much lower representation for older content.

Interpretation¶

Netflix appears to rely heavily on modern content, which aligns with changing audience preferences and the platform’s focus on keeping its catalog fresh and relevant.

In [42]:
# Content Rating Distribution
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.countplot(data=df_clean, y='rating', order=df_clean['rating'].value_counts().index)
plt.title('Distribution of Content Ratings')
plt.xlabel('Count')
plt.ylabel('Rating')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observation¶

Ratings such as TV-MA and TV-14 appear most frequently in the dataset.

Interpretation¶

This suggests that Netflix’s catalog is strongly oriented toward teen and adult audiences, though it still includes family-friendly and children’s content.

In [43]:
# Movie Duration Distribution
movie_data = df_clean[df_clean['duration_type'] == 'Minutes']

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.histplot(movie_data['duration_int'].dropna(), bins=30, kde=True)
plt.title('Distribution of Movie Duration (in Minutes)')
plt.xlabel('Duration (Minutes)')
plt.ylabel('Number of Movies')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observation¶

Most movies appear to cluster around standard feature-film lengths, with fewer very short or very long titles.

Interpretation¶

Netflix’s movie catalog largely follows conventional movie duration norms, which may reflect audience preferences for standard-length films.

In [44]:
# Titles Added by Month
month_order = ['January', 'February', 'March', 'April', 'May', 'June',
               'July', 'August', 'September', 'October', 'November', 'December']

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))
sns.countplot(data=df_clean, x='month_added', order=month_order)
plt.title('Number of Titles Added by Month')
plt.xlabel('Month Added')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observation¶

The number of titles added varies across months, indicating that Netflix does not add content uniformly throughout the year.

Interpretation¶

This may help identify stronger content release windows and can support decisions around the timing of new title launches.

In [45]:
# Bivariate Visual Analysis
# Movies vs TV Shows Added Over Time
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.countplot(data=df_clean, x='year_added', hue='type', order=sorted(df_clean['year_added'].dropna().unique()))
plt.title('Movies vs TV Shows Added to Netflix by Year')
plt.xlabel('Year Added')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observation¶

This plot shows how the number of Movies and TV Shows added to Netflix has changed over time.

Interpretation¶

This helps identify whether Netflix has increased its focus on TV Shows in more recent years, which is a key part of the business problem.

In [46]:

plt.title('Top 10 Countries by Number of Titles')
plt.xlabel('Number of Titles')
plt.ylabel('Country')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observation¶

A small number of countries contribute a large share of Netflix’s available content.

Interpretation¶

This suggests that Netflix’s catalog is concentrated in a few major production markets, which can guide both growth strategy and localization decisions.

In [47]:

plt.xlabel('Count')
plt.ylabel('Genre')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Genres such as International Movies, Dramas, and Comedies appear very frequently in the catalog.

Interpretation¶

This indicates that Netflix relies strongly on broad-appeal genres and international content categories, which are likely central to its global growth model.

In [48]:

plt.xlabel('Count')

plt.ylabel('Rating')

plt.show()

This helps identify whether Netflix uses different audience targeting strategies for movies versus serialized content.

# Outlier Check/Spread Analysis
# Movie Distribution BoxPlot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.boxplot(x=movie_data['duration_int'].dropna())
plt.title('Boxplot of Movie Duration')
plt.xlabel('Duration (Minutes)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observation¶

The boxplot helps identify the spread of movie durations and reveals whether extreme-duration outliers are present.

Interpretation¶

A few very short or very long movies may exist, but most titles are concentrated within a standard runtime range.

In [52]:
# TV Show Season Boxplot
tv_data = df_clean[df_clean['duration_type'] == 'Seasons']

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.boxplot(x=tv_data['duration_int'].dropna())
plt.title('Boxplot of Number of Seasons for TV Shows')
plt.xlabel('Number of Seasons')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observation¶

Most TV Shows appear to have a relatively low number of seasons, while a few titles extend much longer.

Interpretation¶

This suggests that Netflix’s TV catalog is dominated by short-run series, which may indicate a preference for limited-series formats or lower renewal risk.

In [53]:
# Heatmap of the content type by year added
content_year = pd.crosstab(df_clean['year_added'], df_clean['type'])

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
sns.heatmap(content_year, annot=True, fmt='g', cmap='YlGnBu')
plt.title('Heatmap of Content Type by Year Added')
plt.xlabel('Type')
plt.ylabel('Year Added')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observation¶

The heatmap provides a compact view of how Movies and TV Shows were added across different years.

Interpretation¶

This makes it easier to identify years in which Netflix accelerated content expansion and whether the growth pattern differed between movies and TV shows.

Missing Value and Outlier Check¶

In this section, the goal is to examine whether missing values and outliers could materially affect the quality of the analysis.

Since missing value treatment is optional in this case study, the focus will be on:

  • identifying the remaining missing values after preprocessing
  • understanding which columns are affected the most
  • checking whether numerical variables contain extreme values
  • deciding whether any treatment is required before drawing business conclusions

This step is important because missing values and unusual observations can sometimes distort patterns, especially in business-facing analysis.

In [62]:
# Missing values after preprocessing
missing_counts = df_clean.isnull().sum()
missing_counts[missing_counts > 0].sort_values(ascending=False)
Out[62]:
director         2634
country           831
cast              825
date_added         98
year_added         98
month_added        98
rating              4
duration            3
duration_int        3
duration_type       3
dtype: int64

# Missing value percentage after preprocessing
missing_percent = round((df_clean.isnull().sum() / len(df_clean)) * 100, 2)
missing_percent[missing_percent > 0].sort_values(ascending=False)
Out[63]:
director         29.91
country           9.44
cast              9.37
date_added        1.11
year_added        1.11
month_added       1.11
rating            0.05
duration          0.03
duration_int      0.03
duration_type     0.03
dtype: float64
In [56]:
# Visualize missing values count
missing_plot = missing_counts[missing_counts > 0].sort_values(ascending=True)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
missing_plot.plot(kind='barh')
plt.title('Missing Values by Column')
plt.xlabel('Number of Missing Values')
plt.ylabel('Column')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

plt.title('Outlier Check: Movie Duration')
plt.xlabel('Duration (Minutes)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

plt.title('Outlier Check: Number of Seasons for TV Shows')
plt.xlabel('Number of Seasons')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Out[61]:
title duration_int country release_year
548 Grey's Anatomy 17.0 United States 2020
2423 Supernatural 15.0 United States, Canada 2019
4798 NCIS 15.0 United States 2017

7847 Red vs. Blue 13.0 United States 2015
5412 Criminal Minds 12.0 United States, Canada 2017
4964 Trailer Park Boys 12.0 Canada 2018
6456 Cheers 11.0 United States 1992
6795 Frasier 11.0 United States 2003

Observations from Missing Value and Outlier Check¶

1. Missing value analysis¶

The missing value check shows that missing data is concentrated mainly in metadata-related columns rather than the core structural columns of the dataset.

Highest missing columns¶

  • director → 2634 missing values (29.91%)
  • country → 831 missing values (9.44%)
  • cast → 825 missing values (9.37%)

Smaller missing columns¶

  • date_added → 98 missing values (1.11%)
  • year_added → 98 missing values (1.11%)
  • month_added → 98 missing values (1.11%)
  • rating → 4 missing values (0.05%)
  • duration → 3 missing values (0.03%)
  • duration_int → 3 missing values (0.03%)
  • duration_type → 3 missing values (0.03%)

Interpretation of missing values¶

The missing values are mostly concentrated in descriptive metadata such as director, cast, and country. This means the dataset is still strong enough for business analysis because key variables such as:

  • type
  • title
  • release_year

remain complete.

However, the missing values in director, cast, and country may slightly reduce the completeness of people-level or geography-level insights.

The missing values in year_added and month_added are expected because those columns were derived from date_added, so any missingness in date_added naturally carries forward.

2. Outlier analysis for movie duration¶

The boxplot and numerical summary show that movie duration has a clear central concentration, but also contains a number of extreme high-duration outliers.

Movie duration summary¶

  • Count: 6128
  • Mean: 99.58 minutes
  • Median: 98 minutes
  • 25th percentile: 87 minutes
  • 75th percentile: 114 minutes
  • Maximum: 312 minutes

This indicates that most Netflix movies fall within a standard feature-length range, but a few titles are much longer than usual.

Examples of unusually long movies¶

Some extreme cases include:

  • Black Mirror: Bandersnatch → 312 minutes
  • Headspace: Unwind Your Mind → 273 minutes
  • The School of Mischief → 253 minutes
  • No Longer kids → 237 minutes
  • Lock Your Girls In → 233 minutes

Interpretation¶

These values do not appear to be obvious data errors. Instead, they likely represent:

  • interactive specials
  • anthology content
  • older long-format films
  • special editions or unusually long productions

So, while these are statistical outliers, they are still valid content entries and should not be removed without a strong reason.

3. Outlier analysis for TV show seasons¶

The boxplot and summary for TV shows show that the catalog is heavily concentrated around short-run shows, with a few long-running series appearing as outliers.

TV show season summary¶

  • Count: 2676
  • Mean: 1.76 seasons
  • Median: 1 season
  • 25th percentile: 1 season
  • 75th percentile: 2 seasons
  • Maximum: 17 seasons

This tells us that most Netflix TV shows are short in length, and one-season series are especially common.

Examples of long-running shows¶

Some of the longest-running series include:

  • Grey’s Anatomy → 17 seasons
  • Supernatural → 15 seasons
  • NCIS → 15 seasons
  • Heartland → 13 seasons
  • COMEDIANS of the world → 13 seasons
  • Criminal Minds → 12 seasons
  • Trailer Park Boys → 12 seasons
  • Cheers → 11 seasons
  • Frasier → 11 seasons

Interpretation¶

These are not likely to be invalid values. Instead, they represent genuine long-running series, which can be strategically important because such shows often support:

  • audience retention
  • binge-watching behavior
  • long-term engagement

4. Treatment decision¶

No aggressive missing value treatment or outlier removal is applied in this case study.

This is a reasonable choice because:

  • the missing values are mainly in metadata fields
  • the outliers appear plausible and meaningful
  • the analysis is exploratory and business-oriented rather than predictive-model-focused

Removing these values might actually reduce business meaning rather than improve it.

5. Business takeaway¶

From a business perspective, this section suggests that:

  • Netflix’s catalog metadata is incomplete in some areas, especially director and cast information
  • the movie catalog mostly follows standard runtime expectations, with a few special long-format exceptions
  • the TV catalog is dominated by short-run series, but also includes a smaller set of long-running franchises

This diversity in content length is likely useful for serving different viewing preferences, from quick movie sessions to long-term serialized engagement.

Business Insights¶

Based on the non-graphical analysis, visual analysis, and missing value review, several important business insights emerge from Netflix’s content catalog.

These insights aim to answer the central business question of this case study:

What type of shows or movies should Netflix focus on producing, and how can the platform grow its business across different countries?

1. Netflix’s catalog is still dominated by movies¶

The analysis shows that nearly 70% of the catalog consists of movies, while TV shows account for about 30%.

Business meaning¶

This suggests that movies remain the largest content category on the platform and continue to form the backbone of Netflix’s library.

Strategic implication¶

Netflix should continue investing in movies, but not uniformly. Instead, it should focus on movie categories that already show strong platform presence and broad audience appeal, especially:

  • dramas
  • comedies
  • international movies
  • documentaries
  • action and adventure titles

2. Netflix relies heavily on relatively recent content¶

The release year analysis shows that the catalog is concentrated in recent years, with a median release year of 2017 and a strong concentration in the last decade.

Business meaning¶

Netflix appears to compete strongly on freshness and relevance rather than relying mainly on old back-catalog content.

Strategic implication¶

This suggests that users likely expect contemporary content. Netflix should continue prioritizing:

  • recent releases
  • new originals
  • modern storytelling formats
  • faster refresh cycles in high-demand genres

3. Mature audience content is a major strength of the platform¶

Ratings such as TV-MA and TV-14 dominate the dataset.

Business meaning¶

A large share of Netflix’s library is targeted toward teen and adult audiences, which likely reflects stronger engagement in these segments.

Strategic implication¶

Netflix should continue strengthening mature and youth-adult content categories, especially in genres like:

  • drama
  • thriller
  • crime
  • action
  • dark comedy
  • documentary

At the same time, the platform should maintain a balanced pipeline of family-friendly content to avoid becoming too narrow in audience focus.

3. Mature audience content is a major strength of the platform¶

Ratings such as TV-MA and TV-14 dominate the dataset.

Business meaning¶

A large share of Netflix’s library is targeted toward teen and adult audiences, which likely reflects stronger engagement in these segments.

Strategic implication¶

Netflix should continue strengthening mature and youth-adult content categories, especially in genres like:

  • drama
  • thriller
  • crime
  • action
  • dark comedy
  • documentary

At the same time, the platform should maintain a balanced pipeline of family-friendly content to avoid becoming too narrow in audience focus.

5. India appears to be a highly important content market¶

The country-level and cast-level analysis shows strong representation from Indian content and Indian actors, including names such as:

  • Anupam Kher
  • Shah Rukh Khan
  • Akshay Kumar
  • Amitabh Bachchan
  • Om Puri

Business meaning¶

India is not just a large audience market; it is also a major content supply market within Netflix’s catalog.

Strategic implication¶

Netflix should continue investing in India through:

  • original films
  • limited series
  • regional-language content
  • partnerships with well-known Indian actors, writers, and directors

India appears to offer both scale and cultural diversity, making it one of the most strategically valuable markets for content expansion.

6. Short-run TV shows dominate the catalog¶

The season analysis shows that most TV shows have only 1 or 2 seasons, while a smaller number run much longer.

Business meaning¶

Netflix’s catalog is heavily weighted toward short-run or limited-series style content rather than extremely long franchise-heavy television.

Strategic implication¶

This may reflect a deliberate strategy:

  • lower renewal risk
  • faster experimentation
  • easier audience entry
  • shorter production commitment

Netflix should continue using short-run series as a flexible format for testing genres, regions, and audience segments. At the same time, when a show performs strongly, it may be worth extending it into a long-term franchise because long-running series can improve retention and repeat viewing.

7. Standard-length movies appear to be the norm¶

The movie duration analysis shows that most films fall into a conventional runtime range, with a median close to 98 minutes.

Business meaning¶

Netflix’s catalog mostly aligns with normal feature-film viewing behavior. Most users are likely being served content that fits standard entertainment time expectations.

Strategic implication¶

Netflix should continue producing and acquiring movies in the standard-length range because that appears to be the most commercially practical format. Long-duration titles should be used selectively for:

  • special projects
  • prestige content
  • event-driven releases
  • anthology or experimental formats

8. Metadata gaps may slightly weaken some people-level or country-level analysis¶

The missing value analysis shows substantial missingness in:

  • director
  • cast
  • country

Business meaning¶

Some business conclusions related to creator ecosystems, actor frequency, or geography may slightly undercount certain titles.

Strategic implication¶

Netflix could improve internal metadata consistency because stronger metadata quality would support:

  • better recommendation systems
  • better audience segmentation
  • stronger production intelligence
  • more accurate talent and regional analysis

9. Netflix appears to be balancing breadth with localization¶

The platform contains:

  • globally popular genres such as dramas and comedies
  • regionally important content markets
  • a strong mix of movies, TV shows, documentaries, and international categories

Business meaning¶

Netflix is not relying on a single content formula. Instead, it appears to be balancing broad-appeal global content with region-specific offerings.

Strategic implication¶

This suggests that Netflix’s strongest growth model is likely:

  • globally scalable genres
  • locally resonant storytelling
  • content diversification by geography and audience type

Overall Insight Summary¶

Overall, the analysis suggests that Netflix’s content strategy is strongest when it combines:

  • a movie-heavy catalog base
  • strong mature-audience offerings
  • global and international content expansion
  • short-run TV show experimentation
  • selective long-running franchises
  • strong investment in major international markets such as India and other high-output regions

These insights form the basis for the final recommendations.

Recommendations¶

1. Continue investing in movies, but focus on high-performing genres¶

Since movies make up the majority of the catalog, Netflix should continue using films as a core content pillar. However, investment should be more selective and concentrated in genres that already show strong representation and broad appeal, especially:

  • dramas
  • comedies
  • international movies
  • documentaries
  • action and adventure

These categories appear to form a strong base for audience reach and engagement.

2. Expand international content production aggressively¶

International Movies and International TV Shows are among the strongest categories in the dataset, and multiple non-US countries contribute significantly to the catalog.

Netflix should continue increasing investment in:

  • local originals
  • multilingual content
  • region-specific storytelling
  • international licensing partnerships

This is especially relevant in markets such as:

  • India
  • United Kingdom
  • Japan
  • South Korea
  • Spain
  • France

These regions appear to offer strong opportunities for both audience growth and content supply.

3. Strengthen content strategy in India¶

India stands out as one of the most important markets in the dataset, both in country-level representation and in repeated appearance of major actors.

Netflix should deepen its India strategy through:

  • Hindi originals
  • regional-language films and series
  • partnerships with top Indian actors, directors, and creators
  • strong promotion of Indian content to both domestic and global audiences

India appears to be one of the most scalable markets for future content expansion.

4. Keep building for mature audiences, while preserving catalog balance¶

The dominance of TV-MA and TV-14 suggests that Netflix’s strongest content base is in teen and adult-oriented categories.

Netflix should continue building in mature-content segments such as:

  • crime
  • thriller
  • drama
  • dark comedy
  • serious documentaries

However, the company should also maintain enough:

  • family content
  • children’s programming
  • PG and PG-13 catalog depth

This is important to avoid overconcentration in one audience segment.

5. Use short-run TV shows as a low-risk experimentation model¶

Most TV shows in the catalog have only 1 or 2 seasons. This suggests that shorter series may already be functioning as a flexible format for testing content ideas.

Netflix should continue using short-run shows to:

  • test new genres
  • test new markets
  • test audience response
  • reduce renewal risk
  • introduce fresh creators and talent

This strategy is likely efficient for discovering successful concepts before committing to long-running productions.

6. Extend selected hits into long-running franchises¶

Although most TV shows are short, the dataset also includes a small set of long-running series.

This suggests that long-running content still has strategic value when a title performs strongly.

Netflix should identify high-performing series and selectively extend them into:

  • multi-season franchises
  • spin-offs
  • universe-based storytelling
  • recurring audience-retention formats

This can improve long-term engagement and subscription retention.

7. Maintain standard-length movie formats for mainstream releases¶

Most movies are clustered around conventional feature-length durations.

Netflix should continue prioritizing standard-length films because they are likely easier for audiences to consume and fit more naturally into everyday viewing behavior.

Very long films should be reserved for:

  • event titles
  • prestige projects
  • special releases
  • experimental formats

8. Improve metadata quality across director, cast, and country fields¶

The missing-value analysis shows that some important metadata fields are incomplete, especially:

  • director
  • cast
  • country

Netflix should improve metadata consistency because stronger metadata would support:

  • better content recommendations
  • more accurate audience segmentation
  • stronger regional strategy
  • better talent and creator analysis
  • improved internal decision-making

9. Balance global scale with local relevance¶

The overall pattern of the catalog suggests that Netflix performs best when it combines:

  • globally popular content categories
  • strong international expansion
  • local storytelling adapted to specific markets

Netflix should continue growing with a dual strategy:

  • scale content globally where possible
  • localize content deeply where necessary

This balance is likely one of the company’s strongest long-term competitive advantages.

Conclusion¶

This case study explored Netflix’s content catalog using data exploration, preprocessing, non-graphical analysis, visual analysis, and business interpretation.

The analysis showed that Netflix’s catalog is dominated by movies, strongly concentrated in recent years, and heavily oriented toward mature audience content. It also revealed that international content plays a major role in the platform’s library, with countries such as the United States, India, and the United Kingdom making major contributions.

Genre-level analysis showed strong representation of International Movies, Dramas, Comedies, and International TV Shows, while duration analysis showed that most movies follow standard feature-film lengths and most TV shows are short-run series with one or two seasons.

From a business point of view, the results suggest that Netflix should continue:

  • investing in high-performing movie genres
  • expanding international and multilingual content
  • strengthening key markets such as India
  • using short-run TV shows for experimentation
  • extending selected successful shows into long-running franchises
  • improving metadata quality for better strategic decision-making

Overall, the findings suggest that Netflix’s growth strategy is strongest when it combines global scale, local relevance, broad genre coverage, and flexible content formats.
